## Overview

Towns Protocol is an open-source, decentralized encrypted chat protocol with an EVM L2 chain + custom chat nodes, smart contracts, and on-chain memberships. Towns is an open messaging protocol built on Base that powers private groups and decentralized finance.

## Installation

To use @towns-protocol/sdk in your project, run `npm i @towns-protocol/sdk`. The latest version is 0.0.286.

## Getting Started Tutorial

The official documentation provides a React SDK getting started guide that covers:

1. **Connection Setup**: You can configure Wagmi with WagmiConfig and TownsSyncProvider from the react-sdk to set up your app.

2. **Connection Methods**: You can connect to Towns using either a Web3 wallet (recommended) or a bearer token. You can use the following networks: omega - The Towns mainnet, uses Base as the EVM chain · gamma - The Towns testnet, uses Base Sepolia as the EVM chain. We recommend using gamma as a starting point.

3. **Creating Spaces**: You can only create a space with a Web3 wallet. If you're using a bearer token, you can't create spaces.

## Key Resources

River documentation is available at https://docs.towns.com/introduction, along with resources on River Messaging with encryption and the Towns Encryption protocol.

For more detailed tutorials and API documentation, visit the official [Towns documentation](https://docs.towns.com/) and the [npm package page](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@towns-protocol/sdk).

Here's what I found regarding the Towns Protocol SDK documentation and 2026 developer integration:

## Official Documentation & SDK Resources

**Primary Docs:** The River documentation is available at https://docs.towns.com/introduction, with messaging encryption docs at https://docs.towns.com/concepts/encryption and encryption protocol details at https://docs.towns.com/build/towns-encryption. The GitHub repository is at https://github.com/towns-protocol/towns

**NPM Package:** The official SDK is published as `@towns-protocol/sdk` on NPM.

**Developer Hub:** The Towns Developer Hub (towns.com/developer-hub) offers developer resources, quick starts, and academy modules. With wallets natively integrated into chat, developers can build on an open, on-chain social layer where bots share identity, reputation, and state across communities. The hub includes comprehensive tutorials for bot building, daily live streams, weekly prize competitions, and hackathon events.

## Protocol Overview

Towns Protocol is an open source protocol for building decentralized real-time messaging apps. It consists of an EVM-compatible L2 chain, decentralized off-chain stream nodes, and smart contracts deployed on Base. Towns allows people to create programmable communication use cases ("Spaces") in a permissionless manner, featuring ownable Spaces, on-chain subscriptions ("Memberships"), an extendable reputation system, and end-to-end message encryption.

Towns provides developers with a full SDK to integrate programmable chat into any Web3 application, DAO, or NFT project, making it a foundational piece of decentralized social infrastructure.

## 2026 Developer Integration Plans

**Hackathons & Adoption:** Towns plans hackathons in 2026 to accelerate developer adoption, targeting bots, client interfaces, and monetization tools. These events aim to expand use cases like token-gated chat analytics and NFT-based membership systems.

**SDK Improvements:** Upcoming SDK improvements aim to simplify building Towns-compatible apps, with a focus on mobile optimization and AI integration for automated moderation/engagement.

**Grants Program:** The protocol is prioritizing bots that enable onchain actions (e.g., Towns Wars' tokenized battles), with grants up to $250K for projects blending gaming, DeFi, and social features.

**Recent Contract Updates:** Smart contracts governing Space memberships (ERC-721) gained modular reputation-tracking features and cross-chain entitlement checks. Developers can now implement custom staking requirements for Space access directly in membership NFTs, with gas savings of ~12% per mint.

## Smart Contract Integration

Towns contracts are composable, extensible, and upgradable. Developers can use them to set community rules, define custom business models, and craft their own vision of the Towns protocol. Towns uses a factory model to create and manage spaces, where each space gets deployed to its own contract address. Key functions include `createSpace`, `createChannel`, `createRole`, `addPermissionToRole`, and entitlement management for access control.

# Towns Protocol: Key Concepts

## Spaces
Spaces are the core communication units in Towns Protocol. They are "ownable, feature on-chain subscriptions ('Memberships'), an extendable reputation system, and end-to-end message encryption." Space creators own them as onchain assets and can customize access rules through programmable interfaces integrated with external EVM-compatible contracts.

## Memberships
The protocol requires users to hold valid membership tokens to send and receive messages in a Space. "Paid memberships enforce a protocol fee used to pay for the operation of the network, while free Spaces do not incur protocol fees." This creates a sustainable economic model for network operation.

## Architecture: Chain vs. Nodes
Towns employs a two-layer architecture:
- **Smart Contracts on Base**: Deployed on Ethereum's Layer 2, enabling space creation and management with customizable access controls
- **Towns Protocol & Stream Nodes**: "The backbone for social messaging" comprising an app chain and decentralized off-chain stream nodes that "collectively manag[e] encrypted communications across the network"

This separation allows the app chain to optimize for speed while maintaining security through Base's proven infrastructure.

# Towns Protocol Repository Overview

## Repository Structure

The Towns Protocol repository contains:

**Key Directories:**
- `/core` - Core protocol implementation
- `/packages` - NPM packages and libraries
- `/protocol` - Protocol definitions
- `/infra` - Infrastructure code
- `/scripts` - Build and utility scripts
- `/.claude` - Claude-specific configuration

**Configuration Files:**
- `package.json` - Node.js dependencies
- `turbo.json` - Monorepo build configuration
- `bunfig.toml` - Bun runtime settings
- `go.work` - Go workspace configuration

## Project Description

Towns Protocol is "a permissionless, decentralized end to end encrypted chat network." Key features include:

- "end-to-end encrypted messaging - privacy by design"
- "distributed, decentralized infrastructure - no single point of failure"
- "programmable spaces - customizable on-chain communities"
- "cross-chain ready - multi-chain permission system"

The protocol is "built on Base with an EVM-compatible L2 chain" and enables creation of "programmable communication spaces with end-to-end encryption."

## Technology Stack

**Languages:** Go (34.5%), TypeScript (32.1%), Solidity (28.9%), with MDX, Shell, and JavaScript

    62→## Identity Metadata (ERC-8004)
    63→
    64→Bots can optionally define ERC-8004 compliant identity metadata for agent discovery and trust.

    66→### Minimal Example
    67→

    76→  image: "https://example.com/bot-logo.png",

content.content:
# Towns Bot Getting Started Guide

## Bot Creation & Credentials

Visit the [Developer Portal](https://app.towns.com/developer) and create a new bot. You'll receive two essential credentials:

- **APP_PRIVATE_DATA**: "Your bot's private key and encryption device (base64 encoded string)"
- **JWT_SECRET**: "Used to verify webhook requests from Towns servers"

Store these securely for deployment.

## Development Setup

Initialize your project using the Towns CLI:

```bash
bunx towns-bot init my-bot
cd my-bot
bun install
```

Add the required bot discovery endpoint to `src/index.ts`:

```ts
app.get('/.well-known/agent-metadata.json', async (c) => {
  return c.json(await bot.getIdentityMetadata())
})
```

Configure your environment by copying `.env.sample` to `.env`, then push your code to GitHub.

## Deployment (Render.com)

1. Sign up at render.com with GitHub login
2. Create a Web Service connected to your bot repository
3. Set build command to `bun install` and start command to `bun run start`
4. Add environment variables:
   - `APP_PRIVATE_DATA`
   - `JWT_SECRET`
   - `PORT`: 5123

Render provides a public URL like `https://my-bot.onrender.com`.

## Configuration in Towns

Set your webhook URL to `https://my-bot.onrender.com/webhook` in the developer dashboard. Choose your forwarding settings (All Messages, Mentions/Commands/Replies/Reactions, or No Messages), then install the bot to a space.

## Wallet Architecture

Your bot operates two wallets:
- **Bot Treasury**: Holds tips and payments (`bot.appAddress`)
- **Gas Wallet**: Pays transaction fees; must be funded with Base ETH to execute operations

# Onchain Integrations for Towns Bots

## Smart Contract Interactions

Towns bots function as onchain applications with two key capabilities: direct contract interactions via the bot's wallet and requesting user transaction/message signatures.

### Bot Wallet Architecture

Bots operate with dual addresses:
- **Gas wallet** (`bot.viem.account`): Signs transactions and covers fees
- **App address** (`bot.appAddress`): Functions as a SimpleAccount treasury wallet

The gas wallet requires Base ETH to cover transaction costs.

## Reading and Writing to Contracts

**Reading contract state** requires no gas costs and uses the `readContract` function to query contract data directly.

**Writing to contracts** leverages the ERC-7821 `execute` function. As documented, bots can "Use `execute` from ERC-7821 for any onchain interaction" by specifying the target address, ABI, function name, and arguments.

## Batch Operations

Bots support atomic batch transactions—multiple operations that either all succeed or all fail together. This enables complex sequences like approval followed by token swaps in a single transaction.

## User Interactions

For operations requiring user participation (transaction signing, message signatures, or form submissions), bots utilize `sendInteractionRequest` rather than executing directly. This separates bot-initiated operations from user-authorized actions.

## Utility Functions

The `getSmartAccountFromUserId` function retrieves a user's smart account address, enabling use cases like token distribution, airdrops, balance checks, and ownership verification.

## RPC Configuration

Custom RPC endpoints are recommended to avoid rate limits on public endpoints, with Alchemy cited as a reputable provider option.

content.content:
# Interaction Requests in Towns

## Overview

The Towns platform enables bots to send interactive UI elements through `handler.sendInteractionRequest()`, which accepts a channel ID and payload defining the interaction type.

## Three Interaction Types

**Forms (Buttons & Text Inputs)**

Forms present clickable buttons and text input fields for user responses. According to the documentation, these work well for "menus, confirmations, polls, feedback collection, and multi-step flows." The `recipient` parameter targets a specific user; omitting it makes the interaction publicly available.

Example structure:
```ts
await handler.sendInteractionRequest(event.channelId, {
  type: 'form',
  id: 'my-form',
  components: [
    { id: 'name', type: 'textInput', placeholder: 'Enter name...' },
    { id: 'confirm', type: 'button', label: 'Submit' }
  ],
  recipient: event.userId
})
```

**Transaction Requests**

These prompts request wallet signatures and blockchain execution for payments, token transfers, NFT minting, and DeFi operations. The `tx.signerWallet` parameter can enforce a specific wallet or allow user selection.

**Signature Requests**

These request cryptographic signatures without executing transactions, supporting EIP-712 typed data or personal signatures for authentication and off-chain agreements.

## Handling Responses

Use `bot.onInteractionResponse()` to process user interactions. The callback accesses response type via `response.payload.content?.case` and contains relevant data like button IDs, text values, transaction hashes, or signature results.

The documentation provides a complete poll example demonstrating form creation, response handling, and duplicate vote prevention.

# Local Development Setup for Towns Bots

## Environment Variables

The documentation requires two key variables in your `.env` file:
- `APP_PRIVATE_DATA`
- `JWT_SECRET`

No example configuration is provided in the source material.

## ngrok Tunnel Setup

To expose your local bot to Towns' webhook system:

1. **Start your bot locally** using `bun run dev` on port 5123
2. **Install ngrok** from https://ngrok.com/download
3. **Create the tunnel** by running `ngrok http 5123`
4. **Capture the public URL** from ngrok output (format: `https://abc123.ngrok-free.app`)

The documentation notes: "The free tier of ngrok provides a random URL each time you restart."

## Testing Locally

Update your webhook URL in the Developer Portal to: `https://[your-ngrok-url]/webhook`

Then test by:
- Installing your bot in a test space
- Sending messages or using slash commands
- Monitoring incoming events in your terminal

The guide mentions that "request logging" is included by default, displaying events like: `[2025-10-28 10:30:45] POST /webhook 200 - 45ms`

For deeper debugging, run `DEBUG=csb:bot bun run dev`.

## Production Handoff

Before deploying, update the webhook URL to your production endpoint and remove the ngrok tunnel reference.

The example demonstrates a basic bot implementation using the Towns Protocol. Here's the complete source:

The bot initializes with `makeTownsBot()` using environment variables for authentication. It implements two slash commands:

- **`/help`**: Returns "Available Commands" listing `/help`, `/time`, message triggers like mentions, and reactions
- **`/time`**: Responds with "Current time: [timestamp] ⏰"

- Detects "hello" → replies "Hello there! 👋"
- Detects "ping" → calculates latency and responds "Pong! 🏓 [ms]"
- Detects "react" → adds a thumbs-up reaction

Additionally, it responds to wave emoji (👋) reactions with a message acknowledging the gesture.

The bot is exported as a default app instance after calling `bot.start()`. This demonstrates core bot functionality: slash commands, message parsing, reactions, and event handling within the Towns Protocol ecosystem.

content.content:
# Towns Protocol Bot Events Reference

## Complete Event List

The documentation outlines seven primary event listeners available to bots:

### 1. **onMessage**
Triggered when a message is sent in a channel where the bot operates.

**Payload fields:**
- `message`: Decrypted message text
- `replyId`: Event ID of replied message (if applicable)
- `threadId`: Event ID of thread's first message (if in thread)
- `mentions`: Array of mentioned users with addresses and display names
- `isMentioned`: Boolean indicating bot mention status

**Note:** "Subject to forwarding settings" - availability depends on bot configuration.

### 2. **onSlashCommand**
Fires when users invoke slash commands registered to the bot.

**Payload fields:**
- `command`: Invoked command name
- `args`: Command arguments passed by user
- `mentions`: Array of mentioned users
- `replyId`: Message being replied to (if any)
- `threadId`: Thread context (if any)

### 3. **onReaction**
Triggered when emoji reactions are added to messages.

**Payload fields:**
- `reaction`: Reaction emoji
- `messageId`: Event ID of reacted message

⚠️ **Important limitation:** "You only get the reaction and messageId - not the message content!"

### 4. **onMessageEdit**
Fires when messages are edited.

**Payload fields:**
- `message`: New message content
- `refEventId`: Event ID of original message
- `replyId`: If edited message was a reply
- `threadId`: If edited message was in thread
- `mentions`: Mentioned users in edited message
- `isMentioned`: Bot mention status in edit

### 5. **onRedaction**
Triggered when messages are deleted by users.

**Payload fields:**
- `refEventId`: Event ID of deleted message

### 6. **onEventRevoke**
Fires when messages are revoked by originators or admins with `Redact` permission.

**Payload fields:**
- `refEventId`: Event ID of revoked message

**Availability:** "All Messages" mode only

### 7. **onTip**
Triggered when cryptocurrency tips are sent on messages.

**Payload fields:**
- `messageId`: Event ID receiving tip
- `senderAddress`: Tipper's address
- `receiverAddress`: Recipient's address
- `amount`: Tip amount
- `currency`: Tip currency

**Availability:** "All Messages" mode only

### 8. **onChannelJoin**
Fires when users join channels.

**Payload fields:**
- `userId`: User joining
- `channelId`: Target channel

**Availability:** "All Messages" mode only

### 9. **onChannelLeave**
Triggered when users leave channels.

**Payload fields:**
- `userId`: User departing
- `channelId`: Channel exited

**Availability:** "All Messages" mode only

## Base Payload Structure

All events inherit these core fields:
- `userId`: Event-triggering user
- `spaceId`: Event space (null for DM channels)
- `channelId`: Event channel
- `eventId`: Unique event identifier
- `createdAt`: Event timestamp
- `isDm`: DM channel flag

# Creating and Managing Roles in Towns Protocol

## Role Creation Basics

To establish a role, use the `createRole` method with a name, permission array, and optional user assignments:

```ts
const { roleId } = await bot.createRole(spaceId, {
  name: 'Moderator',
  permissions: [Permission.Read, Permission.Write, Permission.ModifyBanning],
  users: ['0x...', '0x...']
})
```

**Key requirement:** "Role management requires your bot to have admin permissions" and all operations are blockchain transactions requiring gas fees.

## Token-Gated and NFT-Gated Roles

Implement access control using the Rules API by specifying token holdings:

```ts
const townsHolderRule = Rules.checkErc20({
  chainId: 8453n,
  contractAddress: '0x00000000A22C618fd6b4D7E9A335C4B96B189a38',
  threshold: 1n
})

const role = await bot.createRole(spaceId, {
  name: 'Towns Holder',
  permissions: [Permission.Read, Permission.Write],
  rule: townsHolderRule
})

await bot.addRoleToChannel(channelId, role.roleId)
```

Available token-gating options include ERC721 NFTs, ERC20 tokens, ERC1155 assets, ETH balances, and cross-chain entitlements.

## Rule Combinations

Combine multiple conditions using logical operators:

```ts
const andRule = Rules.and(nftRule, tokenRule)  // Both required
const orRule = Rules.or(nftRule, ethRule)      // Either passes
const allRule = Rules.every(ruleA, ruleB)      // All must pass
const anyRule = Rules.some(ruleA, ruleB)       // Any can pass
```

## Role Management Operations

Retrieve, modify, or remove roles:

```ts
const roles = await bot.getAllRoles(spaceId)
const role = await bot.getRole(spaceId, roleId)
await bot.updateRole(spaceId, roleId, { name, users, permissions })
await bot.deleteRole(spaceId, roleId)
```

# Towns Protocol Gamified Chat Bot — Implementation Plan

## Context

The client currently uses **ThirdWeb Engine** for backend NFT minting and wants to add a **gamified chat** layer using **Towns Protocol** — a decentralized, E2E-encrypted messaging protocol built on Base L2. The goal is a working demo that:

- Awards XP for chat activity (messages, reactions, receiving tips)
- Mints achievement NFT badges via ThirdWeb Engine when users hit milestones
- Gates Towns roles by NFT ownership (on-chain entitlements)
- Avoids database calls — uses in-memory state + Towns stream events as a durable ledger + on-chain NFTs for permanence

**Why this approach fits:** Towns Protocol is designed exactly for this — it has native on-chain tipping, NFT-gated roles (`Rules.checkErc721`), webhook-driven bots, and streams that act as an event log. Combined with ThirdWeb Engine for gasless minting, the entire state model lives on blockchain infrastructure.

---

## Architecture Overview

```
┌─────────────────┐     webhook      ┌──────────────────┐
│  Towns Stream   │ ───────────────► │   Bot (Bun +     │
│  Nodes (E2EE)   │                  │   Hono server)   │
└─────────────────┘                  └────────┬─────────┘
        ▲                                     │
        │ sendMessage/sendGM                  │
        │ createRole (on-chain)               │
        │                                     ▼
┌─────────────────┐                  ┌──────────────────┐
│  Base L2        │ ◄────────────────│  ThirdWeb Engine │
│  (Towns Space   │    mint NFT      │  (your existing  │
│   contracts +   │                  │   setup)         │
│   NFT badges)   │                  └──────────────────┘
└─────────────────┘
```

### State Model (no database)

| Data             | Storage                  | Persistence |
|------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| XP / level       | `Map<userId, PlayerStats>` | Ephemeral (rebuilt from stream on boot) |
| XP deltas        | Towns GM events (`bot.sendGM`) | Durable — written to channel stream |
| Badges / ranks   | ERC-721 NFTs on Base     | Permanent on-chain |
| Role access      | Towns `Rules.checkErc721`| Permanent on-chain |

---

## File Structure

```
towns-gamified-chat/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── .env.sample          ← All env vars documented here
├── .gitignore
├── README.md            ← Setup guide: registering bot, webhook, ThirdWeb config
└── src/
    ├── index.ts         ← Entry: makeTownsBot, wire handlers, start Hono
    ├── commands.ts      ← Slash command definitions (/stats, /leaderboard, /claim, /setup-roles)
    ├── identity.ts      ← ERC-8004 bot identity metadata
    ├── game.ts          ← GameState class: XP tracking, cooldowns, level calc, stream replay
    ├── thirdweb.ts      ← ThirdWeb Engine HTTP client (mint badge NFT)
    └── schemas.ts       ← Zod schemas for GM (generic message) ledger events
```

---

## Environment Variables

| Variable                       | Source                            | Purpose                                      |
|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| `APP_PRIVATE_DATA`             | [Towns Dev Portal](https://app.towns.com/developer) | Bot's private key + encryption device (base64) |
| `JWT_SECRET`                   | Towns Dev Portal                  | Verifies webhook requests from Towns nodes   |
| `PORT`                         | You (default `5123`)              | HTTP server port                             |
| `THIRDWEB_ENGINE_URL`          | Your ThirdWeb Engine instance     | e.g. `https://engine.yourdomain.com`         |
| `THIRDWEB_ENGINE_ACCESS_TOKEN` | ThirdWeb Engine dashboard         | Bearer token for Engine API                  |
| `THIRDWEB_BACKEND_WALLET`      | ThirdWeb Engine dashboard         | Wallet address that signs mint transactions  |
| `BADGE_CONTRACT_ADDRESS`       | Your deployed NFT contract        | ERC-721 on Base for achievement badges       |
| `BADGE_CHAIN_ID`               | `8453` (Base) or `84532` (Sepolia)| Chain where badge contract lives             |
| `GAME_LEDGER_CHANNEL_ID`       | Towns app (after space setup)     | Channel where GM ledger events are written   |
| `BASE_RPC_URL` *(optional)*    | Alchemy/Infura                    | Custom RPC to avoid rate limits              |

---

## Implementation Details

### 1. `src/commands.ts` — Slash Commands

```ts
export default [
  { name: 'stats', description: 'Show your XP and level' },

# Towns Protocol Gamified Chat Bot — Implementation Plan

## Context

The client currently uses **ThirdWeb Engine** for backend NFT minting and wants to add a **gamified chat** layer using **Towns Protocol** — a decentralized, E2E-encrypted messaging protocol built on Base L2. The goal is a working demo that:

- Awards XP for chat activity (messages, reactions, receiving tips)
- Mints achievement NFT badges via ThirdWeb Engine when users hit milestones
- Gates Towns roles by NFT ownership (on-chain entitlements)
- Avoids database calls — uses in-memory state + Towns stream events as a durable ledger + on-chain NFTs for permanence

**Why this approach fits:** Towns Protocol is designed exactly for this — it has native on-chain tipping, NFT-gated roles (`Rules.checkErc721`), webhook-driven bots, and streams that act as an event log. Combined with ThirdWeb Engine for gasless minting, the entire state model lives on blockchain infrastructure.

---

## Architecture Overview

```
┌─────────────────┐     webhook      ┌──────────────────┐
│  Towns Stream   │ ───────────────► │   Bot (Bun +     │
│  Nodes (E2EE)   │                  │   Hono server)   │
└─────────────────┘                  └────────┬─────────┘
        ▲                                     │
        │ sendMessage/sendGM                  │
        │ createRole (on-chain)               │
        │                                     ▼
┌─────────────────┐                  ┌──────────────────┐
│  Base L2        │ ◄────────────────│  ThirdWeb Engine │
│  (Towns Space   │    mint NFT      │  (your existing  │
│   contracts +   │                  │   setup)         │
│   NFT badges)   │                  └──────────────────┘
└─────────────────┘
```

### State Model (no database)

| Data             | Storage                  | Persistence |
|------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| XP / level       | `Map<userId, PlayerStats>` | Ephemeral (rebuilt from stream on boot) |
| XP deltas        | Towns GM events (`bot.sendGM`) | Durable — written to channel stream |
| Badges / ranks   | ERC-721 NFTs on Base     | Permanent on-chain |
| Role access      | Towns `Rules.checkErc721`| Permanent on-chain |

```
towns-gamified-chat/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── .env.sample          ← All env vars documented here
├── .gitignore

input.prompt: Extract how to embed a Towns chat UI in a React application: setup of TownsSyncProvider, connecting with a wallet, displaying channels, and whether this can be mounted at a route like /chat.

content.content:
# Embedding Towns Chat UI in React

## Provider Setup

The foundation requires wrapping your application with two providers. First, configure Wagmi for Web3 wallet connections, then nest `TownsSyncProvider` inside:

> "TownsSyncProvider - For interacting with Towns Protocol"

This dual-provider structure enables both wallet functionality and Towns Protocol communication.

## Wallet Connection

Use the `useAgentConnection` hook to establish a connection:

> "connect(signer, { townsConfig })"

You'll need to convert a viem wallet client to an ethers signer using `getEthersSigner`, since the Towns SDK uses ethers internally.

## Displaying Channels

Once connected to a space, retrieve and display channels using these hooks:

- `useSpace()` - fetches space metadata and channel IDs
- `useChannel()` - gets individual channel details
- `useTimeline()` - retrieves messages from a specific channel

The example shows rendering a list of user spaces, then displaying the default `#general` channel's messages.

## Route Mounting

The documentation doesn't explicitly address route-based mounting. However, the `ChatApp` component example is self-contained and follows standard React patterns, suggesting it could be mounted at dedicated routes like `/chat` without additional configuration beyond normal React Router setup.

Now I have a complete understanding. Let me clarify things for the user and update the plan.## Key points to clarify:

1. **A Towns Space does NOT live at `/chat` on their website** — it's an on-chain smart contract deployed on Base L2 + a stream registered with Towns stream nodes. It's infrastructure, not a URL route.

2. **What COULD live at `/chat`** is an embedded chat UI using `@towns-protocol/react-sdk` — that's an optional frontend client that reads/writes to the Space's channels. But that's separate from the bot.3. 

So you can actually deploy the Space directly without going through app.towns.com — I'd set up a wallet (either creating a new one or using an existing ThirdWeb-managed wallet), establish the signer context with primary and delegate wallets, then instantiate a SyncAgent with the Towns configuration to handle the deployment.

Once the agent starts, I can call the spaces method to create a new Space, which deploys the Space factory contract and registers it with the stream nodes. Bot registration is also doable through the SDK using AppRegistryDapp, though it requires more configuration — the Dev Portal streamlines this by automatically generating the necessary private data. I'm wondering whether the user wants me to add full setup scripts for both the Space and bot registration to the demo, or if they just need the bot integration piece.

questions.question: Since app.towns.com is down, how much bootstrap tooling do you want in the demo?

options.label: Bot + setup script (Recommended)
options.description: Add a `scripts/setup.ts` that deploys a Space AND registers the bot on-chain using a wallet private key. Writes the resulting `spaceId`, `channelId`, and `APP_PRIVATE_DATA` to .env. Fully self-contained — no Dev Portal needed.

options.label: Bot only
options.description: Just the bot code + docs assuming app.towns.com will be back up. Simpler demo, faster to build, but you'll need the Dev Portal for credentials.

options.label: Bot + Space deploy + React UI
options.description: Everything above plus a minimal `@towns-protocol/react-sdk` page for `/chat` on your website so users can chat without the official Towns app. Significantly bigger scope.

questions.question: For the setup script wallet, what should be the funding source for gas on Base Sepolia?

options.label: Fresh wallet PK in .env (Recommended)
options.description: Setup script uses SPACE_OWNER_PRIVATE_KEY from .env. You fund it manually with Base Sepolia ETH from a faucet. Simple, explicit, testnet-friendly.

options.label: ThirdWeb backend wallet
options.description: Reuse your existing ThirdWeb Engine backend wallet as the space owner. Keeps wallet management consolidated but couples the two systems.
